title: Topic 336: The Psychology of Game Trailers: Hype Engineering
content:
> **Topic 336: The Psychology of Game Trailers: Hype Engineering**

**ABSTRACT**

Game trailers, those dazzling digital sirens, possess an almost supernatural ability to coax our wallets open, often months before a product even exists. This paper introduces the burgeoning, and entirely necessary, academic field of “Hype Engineering,” a sophisticated discipline blending narrative alchemy, psychological manipulation, and a flagrant disregard for the eventual reality of gameplay. Through a completely serious and not-at-all-ironic analysis of cognitive biases, dopamine-hijacking editing techniques, and the strategic deployment of sad, bearded protagonists, we deconstruct the trailer as a weapon of mass anticipation. Our findings reveal a startlingly effective formula for manufacturing desire, a formula so potent it can make a grown adult weep over a two-minute cinematic featuring a CGI squirrel. This paper, therefore, serves as both a field guide for the bewildered consumer and a reluctant ode to the marketers who, against all odds, convince us that this time, it will be different.

**INTRODUCTION**

I remember it like it was yesterday. The lights dimmed, the iconic *BRAAAM* of a blockbuster trailer echoed through the room, and for two and a half glorious minutes, I was a believer. I saw a sprawling open world, a hero with a tragic backstory, and a combat system that looked like a ballet of beautiful violence. I was hooked. I pre-ordered the deluxe edition, bought the season pass, and even considered naming my firstborn after the gruff-but-lovable sidekick. Then, the game came out. The sprawling open world was mostly empty, the hero’s tragic backstory was a single line of dialogue, and the combat system had all the grace of a drunken bear trying to assemble IKEA furniture. I had been had. And I was not alone.

This experience, familiar to gamers everywhere, is the subject of our investigation. We are not merely talking about advertising; we are talking about **Hype Engineering**, a term we have coined to describe the systematic and psychologically-driven process of creating a level of anticipation for a video game that may or may not correlate with the final product. In an industry worth hundreds of billions of dollars, where the success or failure of a single title can make or break a studio, the art of the trailer has been elevated to a science. It is a high-stakes game of perception management, where the goal is not just to inform, but to indoctrinate.

This paper will argue that game trailers are not simply marketing tools, but powerful psychological instruments. They are meticulously crafted to exploit our cognitive biases, trigger our emotional responses, and create a powerful sense of desire that can be difficult to resist. We will explore the techniques used by “hype engineers” to create these potent digital cocktails of excitement, from the narrative structures they employ to the specific editing techniques they use to manipulate our dopamine levels. We will also examine the consequences of this hype, both for consumers who are often left feeling disappointed, and for developers who are forced to contend with the impossible expectations that their own marketing has created. So, grab your tinfoil hats and your wallets, because we are about to take a deep dive into the weird and wonderful world of game trailer psychology.

**LITERATURE REVIEW**

To understand the modern art of Hype Engineering, one must first stand on the shoulders of giants—or, more accurately, on a precarious stack of marketing textbooks, psychology papers, and a surprising number of Reddit threads. The academic world, in its infinite wisdom, has not yet formally recognized “Hype Engineering” as a discipline. However, by cobbling together research from disparate fields, we can begin to see the theoretical underpinnings of this powerful practice. The study of game trailers is, in essence, a study of applied psychology, where every cut, every sound, and every line of dialogue is a calculated move in a high-stakes chess match for the consumer’s attention and disposable income.

One of the most critical areas of research is the interplay between emotion and narrative. Villegas et al. (2023) in their work, “Visual Attention and Emotion Analysis Based on Qualitative Assessment and Eyetracking Metrics—The Perception of a Video Game Trailer,” used eye-tracking technology to demonstrate the powerful link between storytelling in trailers and the emotional responses of viewers [1]. Their research suggests that a trailer's success is not merely in showcasing gameplay mechanics, but in its ability to weave a compelling narrative that resonates with the audience on an emotional level. This is the difference between showing a character swinging a sword and showing a character swinging a sword to protect a loved one. The former is an action; the latter is a story. And as any good hype engineer knows, stories sell.

Of course, hype is not a new phenomenon. The technology research firm Gartner developed the “Hype Cycle” to describe the life cycle of emerging technologies, and it can be adapted with frightening accuracy to the world of video games [2]. The cycle begins with a “Technology Trigger,” or in our case, the announcement of a new game. This is followed by the “Peak of Inflated Expectations,” where the hype is at its most feverish and the trailers are at their most dazzling. Then comes the “Trough of Disillusionment,” when the game is released and the reality of its flaws sets in. Finally, if the game is lucky, it will climb the “Slope of Enlightenment” and reach the “Plateau of Productivity,” where it finds a stable audience and, perhaps, a place in the hearts of gamers. This cycle, as we will see, has played out time and time again in the gaming industry, with devastating and often hilarious results.

Underpinning all of this is a veritable smorgasbord of cognitive biases that trailers are expertly designed to exploit. The **Confirmation Bias**, for example, is the tendency to seek out information that confirms our existing beliefs. Once a trailer has convinced us that a game is going to be amazing, we will actively seek out positive previews and ignore negative ones. **Fear of Missing Out (FOMO)** is another powerful motivator, as the social pressure to be part of the conversation around a new game can be immense. And then there is the **Endowment Effect**, the tendency to overvalue something simply because we own it. By encouraging pre-orders, developers can create a sense of ownership in players before the game is even released, making them more invested in its success and less likely to be critical of its flaws. These are just a few of the psychological tricks of the trade that we will explore in more detail in the following sections. The literature, in its own dry way, provides us with the building blocks of hype. It is up to us, as intrepid researchers, to assemble them into the glorious, terrifying edifice of Hype Engineering.

**ANALYSIS**

Now that we have established the theoretical foundations of Hype Engineering, it is time to roll up our sleeves and dissect the beast itself. A game trailer is not just a random assortment of clips; it is a meticulously constructed piece of psychological warfare, designed to bypass our critical faculties and appeal directly to our lizard brains. To understand how this is achieved, we must first examine the anatomy of a typical trailer.

The modern game trailer is a masterclass in pacing and structure. It often begins with a slow, atmospheric opening, designed to draw the viewer in and establish a sense of mystery. This is followed by a gradual build-up of tension, with the music swelling and the cuts becoming more rapid. Then comes the crescendo, a montage of action-packed moments and emotional climaxes, all set to a thumping soundtrack. And finally, the money shot: a breathtaking reveal, a shocking plot twist, or a single, iconic image that will be seared into the viewer’s memory. This structure is not accidental; it is a carefully calibrated emotional rollercoaster, designed to leave the viewer breathless and desperate for more.

One of the key tools in the hype engineer’s arsenal is the use of sound. The infamous “Inception BRAAAM,” a dramatic, bass-heavy sound effect, has become a cliché in trailer production for a reason: it works. It is a sonic cattle prod, designed to jolt the viewer to attention and signal that something epic is about to happen. But sound design in trailers is more than just loud noises. The choice of music, the use of silence, and the careful mixing of dialogue and sound effects all play a crucial role in shaping the viewer’s emotional experience.

Then there is the editing. The quick-cut montage is a staple of game trailers, and for good reason. By bombarding the viewer with a rapid succession of exciting images, editors can create a sense of energy and dynamism, even if the underlying gameplay is slow and methodical. This technique is particularly effective at creating the illusion of a fast-paced, action-packed experience, even when the game itself is more of a slow burn. It is a form of visual sleight of hand, and it is incredibly effective.

But perhaps the most powerful tool in the hype engineer’s toolkit is the use of narrative. As we have already discussed, stories sell, and game trailers are often miniature masterpieces of storytelling. They introduce us to compelling characters, hint at epic conflicts, and promise a world of adventure and discovery. The “sad dad” trope, for example, has become a popular narrative device in recent years. By featuring a grizzled, world-weary protagonist who is motivated by the love of his child, trailers can tap into a deep well of emotion and create a powerful sense of empathy in the viewer. We are not just buying a game; we are buying into a story.

All of these techniques are designed to do one thing: trigger the release of dopamine in the viewer’s brain. Dopamine is a neurotransmitter that is associated with pleasure and reward, and it plays a crucial role in motivation and addiction. By creating a cycle of anticipation and reward, game trailers can effectively hijack our brain’s reward system, leaving us craving more. This is the neurochemical basis of hype, and it is a powerful force to be reckoned with.

Of course, not all trailers are created equal. Some are honest and accurate representations of the final product, while others are exercises in pure, unadulterated misdirection. The art of the trailer is not just about showing off the best parts of a game; it is also about hiding its flaws. By carefully selecting which footage to show and which to leave on the cutting room floor, hype engineers can create a vision of a game that is far more impressive than the reality. This is the dark art of Hype Engineering, and it is a practice that has led to some of the most infamous controversies in gaming history.

**REAL-WORLD EXAMPLES**

Theory is one thing, but the true genius of Hype Engineering can only be appreciated by examining its real-world applications. The history of video games is littered with the corpses of titles that promised the world and delivered a small, buggy continent. These case studies serve as cautionary tales, monuments to the hubris of marketers and the gullibility of consumers.

Our first and perhaps most infamous exhibit is the saga of *No Man's Sky*. The initial trailers for this ambitious space exploration game were nothing short of breathtaking. They promised a near-infinite universe of procedurally generated planets, teeming with unique flora and fauna. Players would be able to seamlessly fly from space to a planet's surface, engage in epic space battles, and interact with other players in a shared universe. The hype was immense, fueled by a masterful marketing campaign that played on our deepest desires for exploration and discovery. The reality, however, was a different story. At launch, *No Man's Sky* was a lonely, repetitive experience, lacking many of the features that had been promised in the trailers. The backlash was swift and brutal, a textbook example of the “Trough of Disillusionment.” To their credit, the developers at Hello Games have since worked tirelessly to update the game and deliver on their original vision, but the initial launch remains a stark reminder of the dangers of over-promising and under-delivering.

Next, we have *Cyberpunk 2077*, a game that represents both the pinnacle and the nadir of Hype Engineering. The marketing campaign for this futuristic RPG was a masterclass in building anticipation. It had a slick, stylish aesthetic, a compelling world, and the star power of Keanu Reeves. The trailers were works of art, meticulously crafted to create a sense of immersion and excitement. They promised a living, breathing city, a deep and branching narrative, and a level of player freedom that was unprecedented. The hype was so intense that the game became a cultural phenomenon before it was even released. But then, the game came out. While the PC version was a solid, if buggy, experience, the console versions were a technical disaster. The game was plagued with glitches, performance issues, and a general sense of unfinishedness. The backlash was so severe that Sony removed the game from the PlayStation Store, an unprecedented move for a title of this magnitude. *Cyberpunk 2077* is a cautionary tale about the dangers of hubris, a reminder that even the most sophisticated marketing campaign cannot save a product that is not ready for release.

But Hype Engineering is not just the domain of big-budget blockbusters. The indie scene has also had its share of hype-fueled successes and failures. A game like *Undertale*, for example, generated a massive amount of hype through word-of-mouth and a clever marketing campaign that emphasized its unique charm and subversive gameplay. The trailer was simple, but effective, showcasing the game's quirky sense of humor and its innovative combat system. The hype was organic, built on a foundation of genuine excitement and a passionate community. This stands in stark contrast to the top-down, big-budget hype campaigns of games like *No Man's Sky* and *Cyberpunk 2077*. It is a reminder that hype can be a force for good, a way to shine a spotlight on deserving games and bring them to a wider audience. But it is a double-edged sword, and for every *Undertale*, there are a dozen indie games that have been crushed under the weight of their own hype.

**DISCUSSION**

The pervasive and increasingly sophisticated nature of Hype Engineering raises a number of important questions for both consumers and creators. For consumers, the central dilemma is one of trust. In an environment where marketing materials are designed to be as persuasive as possible, how can we make informed decisions about the games we buy? Are we destined to be forever at the mercy of our own dopamine-addled brains, or can we learn to be more critical consumers of game marketing? The answer, I believe, lies in education. By understanding the psychological tricks that are being used against us, we can begin to develop a healthy skepticism towards game trailers. We can learn to look past the slick production values and focus on the actual gameplay being shown. We can learn to be wary of promises that seem too good to be true. And we can learn to wait for reviews from trusted sources before we open our wallets.

For developers, the issue of hype is a double-edged sword. On the one hand, a successful marketing campaign can be the difference between a game that sells millions of copies and a game that languishes in obscurity. On the other hand, the pressure to live up to the hype can be immense. When a game has been marketed as the second coming of Christ, anything less than a transcendent experience is bound to be a disappointment. This can create a toxic cycle of crunch, burnout, and creative compromise, as developers struggle to meet the impossible expectations that their own marketing has created. The challenge for developers is to find a way to generate excitement for their games without setting themselves up for failure. This may mean being more transparent about the development process, being more honest about the features that will and will not be in the final game, and being more willing to manage the expectations of their audience.

Looking to the future, it is clear that Hype Engineering will only become more sophisticated. With the rise of AI-generated content, deepfake technology, and new forms of interactive marketing, the line between advertising and reality will become increasingly blurred. We may soon see trailers that are personalized to our individual tastes and preferences, or trailers that allow us to interact with the game world in real-time. The potential for manipulation is immense, and it will be more important than ever for consumers to be vigilant and for developers to be responsible.

**CONCLUSION**

In the grand, cacophonous circus of the video game industry, the trailer is the barker, the hype man, the snake oil salesman with a twinkle in his eye. It is a promise, a prayer, and, all too often, a beautifully crafted lie. We have seen how Hype Engineering, this strange and wonderful discipline, has evolved from a simple marketing tool into a powerful psychological weapon. We have dissected its methods, from the dopamine-hijacking edits to the emotionally manipulative narratives. We have mourned at the graves of its victims, the games that were crushed under the weight of their own impossible promises. And we have, perhaps, learned a thing or two about ourselves in the process.

So, the next time you find yourself mesmerized by a game trailer, the next time you feel that familiar tingle of anticipation, take a moment to step back and ask yourself: Am I being informed, or am I being indoctrinated? Am I excited about the game, or am I excited about the idea of the game? And most importantly, is that a real-time gameplay capture, or is it just another CGI squirrel? The answers may surprise you. And they may just save you from another case of pre-order regret.
references:
[1] Villegas, E., Fonts, E., Fernández, M., & Fernández-Guinea, S. (2023). Visual Attention and Emotion Analysis Based on Qualitative Assessment and Eyetracking Metrics—The Perception of a Video Game Trailer. *Sensors*, *23*(23), 9573. https://doi.org/10.3390/s23239573

[2] Linden, A., & Fenn, J. (2003). Understanding Gartner's hype cycles. *Strategic Analysis Report*, *5*(4), 1-10.
